exemplar.title:
To what extent are people aware that single-use plastic 
consumption contributes to water pollution in Klang Valley?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent are people aware that single-use plastic 
consumption contributes to water pollution in Klang Valley?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Overall logical sequence from background through to conclusion
Consistent use of environmental terminology and proper formula formatting
Use of headings and numbering provides a recognizable framework
weaknesses: Lengthy raw-data blocks and appendices disrupt flow
Frequent grammatical errors and redundancies reduce concision
References list is inconsistent in style and completeness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is mainly logical and uses appropriate terminology (band 2), but sustained organisation and concision are hindered by unwieldy data presentation and language issues. A score of 2 reflects adequate communication with room for structural and stylistic improvement.

strengths: Research question is clear, focused and maintains coherence throughout the report
Background provides broad, relevant environmental context with effective use of maps
Demonstrates clear understanding of the link between single-use plastics and water pollution
weaknesses: âExtentâ and âawarenessâ are not operationally defined at the outset
COD data trends are described without contextual units or significance
Connections between awareness and measurable water-quality indicators lack a fully logical pathway
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs research question is specific and contextually framed, and the background supplies a strong environmental rationale (band 5â6). However, minor gaps in operational definitions (âextentâ, awareness metrics) and incomplete contextualisation of COD data prevent full marks. A score of 5 reflects high-band performance with room for refinement.

strengths: Design of a repeatable questionnaire with pilot testing and statistical planning
Clear outline of ethical considerations (anonymity, voluntary participation)
weaknesses: Convenience sampling via a single WhatsApp group introduces bias and lacks justification of representativeness
âAwarenessâ variable is not operationalized into measurable indicators
No formal risk assessment matrix for online-privacy and data-breach risks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method is well designed and ethically sound (band 5â6), with clear repeatability and relevant data collection techniques. Sampling strategy and variable operationalisation weaknesses prevent the highest mark. A score of 5 recognises strong method design and ethical outline, but flags issues around bias and definition.

strengths: Discussion contextualises findings with relevant secondary literature
Identifies clear strengths and weaknesses of the method
Suggests meaningful modifications (multi-platform distribution, open-ended items)
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks quantitative assessment of sampling error or confidence intervals
Alternative interpretations and broader implications are not fully explored
Improvement suggestions lack detailed sample-size calculations or demographic quotas
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation addresses conclusion validity, method strengths/weaknesses and proposes substantial improvements (band 3-4). Greater depthâquantitative reliability measures and richer alternative interpretationsâwould achieve full marks. A score of 4 recognises solid discussion with scope for deeper analysis.

strengths: Correct calculation and interpretation of means, standard deviations and p-values
Effective identification of key patterns in awareness across age groups
Good linkage of statistical findings to discussion points
weaknesses: Data presentation omits labels, units and clear captions in several charts
Raw-data tables are cluttered with illegible symbols and overwhelm the narrative
Conclusion overstates causality and ignores contradictory findings
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis is largely accurate and demonstrates understanding of statistical trends (upper band 3-4). However, presentation omissions and an overstated conclusion limit completeness. A score of 4 reflects solid analytical work with some notable gaps in clarity and inference.

strengths: Proposes an age-tailored awareness campaign directly linked to findings
Lists relevant strengths and weaknesses of the proposed solution
weaknesses: Justification is brief and lacks feasibility analysis (resources, stakeholders, metrics)
Does not evaluate unintended consequences or monitoring strategies in depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application is relevant and tied to the data, with some evaluation of pros and cons (band 2). However, weak justification and missing implementation detail prevent higher marks. A score of 2 reflects basic application with limited depth.

strandSelections.reasoning: Environmental terminology (e.g., COD, ANOVA, KAP model) is used correctly, but frequent grammatical errors and redundancy reduce concision. The strand therefore aligns with band 2, where either terminology or concision is achieved but not both consistently.

The student compares findings with literature and recognises large error bars and cultural constraints, partially evaluating how well the conclusion answers the environmental question. Discussion stops short of weighing alternative interpretations or broader implications, fitting solidly in the 3-4 band but not comprehensive.

The conclusion restates that younger groups are more aware and cites mean scores and p-values. While supported by data, it ignores contradictions (no significant practice differences) and overstates causal links. Thus it meets âinterprets trends with some validityâ but lacks full support for the highest band.

Means, standard deviations and p-values are calculated correctly for Likert data, and patterns are commented on for each question. Minor statistical weaknesses (treating Likert means as interval, limited ANOVA explanation) prevent completeness, yet core analysis is largely correct, warranting the upper point in the 3-4 band.

Suggested improvementsâmulti-platform distribution, open-ended questions, clarifying termsâdirectly address identified weaknesses and suggest richer data collection. They are meaningful but not sufficiently detailed (e.g., no sample-size calculations), so the work earns the middle band.

The questionnaire structure, pilot test, Likert scales and basic statistical plan (mean, SD, ANOVA) are described, allowing repetition. Nevertheless, convenience sampling via a WhatsApp group and the absence of an operational definition for âawarenessâ weaken suitability for collecting sufficiently relevant data. Hence the strand meets ârepeatable but insufficientâ slightly above adequate.

Connections between the issue (plastic pollution) and the research question are described: the student claims limited public awareness contributes to Klang River ranking among the worldâs most polluted rivers and that varying awareness affects disposal practices. However, explanation remains qualitative and omits a logical pathway linking awareness to measured water-quality indicators. Because some links are present but not comprehensive, the mid-high band (3-4) fits best.

The student formulates the question "To what extent are people aware that single-use plastic consumption contributes to water pollution in Klang Valley?" The wording is specific to a population, a behaviour (awareness of single-use plastics) and a clearly defined environmental consequence (water pollution in Klang Valley). It is narrow enough to be investigated with a survey and remains consistent throughout the report. Minor typographical errors (e.g. âRESEARHâ) do not undermine focus, so a high-band judgement is appropriate, though not full marks because the phrase âto what extentâ is not operationalised into measurable criteria at the outset.

Strengths (cost-effective, culturally relevant) and weaknesses (resource demands, measuring impact) of the proposed campaign are listed, but evaluation is brief and omits potential unintended consequences or monitoring metrics. This corresponds to âevaluates some relevant strengths and weaknessesâ (2 marks).

Headings and numbering offer a clear framework, yet large raw-data blocks interrupt flow and some tables spill across pages without captions. Organisation is present but not sustained, placing the work in band 2.

Strengths (cost-effectiveness, clear sections) and weaknesses (small, imbalanced sample, online bias) are described, and the student explains how these could affect validity. Limitations such as non-random sampling or questionnaire design bias are not explored exhaustively; therefore the discussion is descriptive rather than fully analytical.

The background section discusses Malaysiaâs growing e-commerce waste, national single-use-plastic policies and presents COD data and a map of Klang Valley. These details establish water quality problems and link them to plastic waste, giving readers a solid environmental frame. Some statistics are out of context (COD trends not fully explained) but the breadth and relevance of information merit the 5-6 band rather than the mid-band.

The student states each age group should have >30 respondents for the Central Limit Theorem and describes distribution through residentsâ WhatsApp. While the strategy is described, it is not random and lacks justification of representativeness; therefore the work demonstrates description, not justification, placing it in the 3-4 range but nearer the lower end.

Ethical considerations (anonymity, voluntary participation, data for sole use) are outlined and the online format entails negligible physical risk. Although a formal risk matrix is missing, coverage is clear enough for an âoutlineâ, fulfilling the top of the 3-4 band.

The student provides tables of raw data, percentage tables and multiple bar charts. However, several tables are cluttered with illegible symbols, and only selected questions are graphed. Titles and axis labels exist but units are not always clear. These omissions align with the âappropriate with some omissionsâ descriptor.

One applicationâan age-tailored awareness campaignâis described with some contextual detail. Justification links to findings (older group less aware) but economic, social or feasibility analysis is thin, matching the âdescribes with weak justificationâ descriptor.

Arguments generally follow a logical sequenceâfrom background, to data, to conclusionâand references are cited; however, digressions (lengthy raw tables, symbol clutter) hinder readability. The narrative is mainly logical but difficult to follow in parts, consistent with band 2.

annotations.comment: The research question is clear and focused but lacks operational definitions for "extent" and quantifiable awareness criteria. Defining measurable indicators would enhance precision and alignment with the investigable scope.

Background section provides relevant e-commerce and plastic life-cycle data, but the COD trend is described without context or units. Explain the significance of COD values and axes for reader clarity.
Figure 1 is informative but lacks clear axis labels and units for COD. Ensure each chart includes labeled axes, units, and a brief description of data sources to improve data presentation.
The map of the study area effectively situates Klang Valley, strengthening context. Consider adding a scale bar and data source citation directly on the map for completeness.
Variables table is comprehensive but the concept of âawarenessâ should be operationalized. Add a definition of awareness levels linked to specific survey items to justify method design.

Convenience sampling via a single WhatsApp group introduces bias and limits representativeness. Justify why this strategy can yield a balanced sample or consider stratified recruitment across different community forums.

The sampling justification cites the Central Limit Theorem but does not explain how age strata were selected. Include rationale for age-group thresholds, sampling quotas, and representativeness justification.
Ethical considerations are outlined, but a formal risk matrix is absent. Create a succinct risk assessment table detailing potential online privacy, data breach, and mitigation strategies.

Table 4 lists all questions and justifications, which is thorough. However, some justification cells are blank. Ensure each survey item has a clear justification linked to knowledge, attitudes, or practice goals.
Raw data tables are comprehensive but overcrowded and contain illegible symbols. Simplify presentation by removing OCR artifacts and summarizing key statistics instead of full raw data.
Figure 3âs caption and discussion restate percentages but the figure axes and legend are not referenced. Cite axis labels in the analysis and discuss data limitations due to sampling imbalance.
Figure 4 interpretation effectively links generational differences to cost concerns. Consider adding numerical values on the chart bars to reinforce comparisons for each age group.
The percentage calculation formula is shown clearly, but the narrative should interpret what 26.67% implies for question A3. Link the example to broader data patterns.
Table 9 is dense and difficult to follow due to merged cells and symbols. Simplify by splitting into separate tables for each age group or key question to enhance clarity.
Mean and SD equations are properly formatted in LaTeX. Including an interpretation sentence directly after each calculation would reinforce the statistical reasoning.
Table 10 effectively summarizes means, SDs, and p-values, but decimal places vary inconsistently. Standardize to two decimal places across all cells.
Analysis of Figure 6 correctly identifies outliers and SD trends. To deepen evaluation, discuss effect sizes or confidence intervals to quantify the magnitude of differences.
Analysis text overstates causality between awareness and age. Clarify that correlations do not imply causation and acknowledge potential confounding variables.

Figure 7 illustrates practice patterns well but the p-value discussion is confusingâpractices were described as nonâsignificant yet previous results had significant p-values. Reconcile these findings.

Conclusion reiterates the hypothesis support but overgeneralizes from neutral mean differences. Instead, discuss the limited effect sizes and acknowledge when differences fall below practical significance.
Discussion uses relevant secondary literature to contextualize conclusions. Strengthen DEV by integrating this literature to critically evaluate anomalies and compare effect sizes.

The evaluation rightfully flags cultural and education biases but lacks quantitative assessment of sampling error. Include a discussion of confidence intervals or margin of error to evaluate reliability.
Improvement suggestions are meaningfulâmulti-platform distribution and open-ended items. For greater depth, propose specific sample-size calculations and targeted demographic quotas.
Application proposal suggests a culturally tailored campaign. To justify feasibility, include resource estimates, stakeholder analysis, and monitoring metrics to evaluate effectiveness.
References list is extensive but inconsistent formatting and missing DOIs hinder credibility. Apply a single citation style (APA/Chicago) and ensure complete entries.

Overall report structure follows IB conventions but lengthy raw-data appendices disrupt flow. Consider moving exhaustive data tables to an appendix and summarizing key findings in the main text for improved logic.
